mnemonics.title: Order of Permutation - LCM Rule
mnemonics.content: Order of permutation is found by taking the LCM of cycle lengths. Remember: 'Long Cycles Make Extraordinary Order' - Longest Cycle = LCM of lengths.

mnemonics.title: Even vs Odd Permutations
mnemonics.content: Even = Identity can be made with Even # of swaps. Odd = Identity needs Odd # of swaps. Mnemonic: 'Evan's OOPS' - Even = Even # transpositions, Odd = Odd # transpositions.

mnemonics.title: Fermat's Little Theorem
mnemonics.content: For prime p, a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p). Think: 'Pretty (p-1) Power gives 1' - FLT = 'First Lasts (a^(p-1) = 1)'.

mnemonics.title: Z*p Order
mnemonics.content: Z*p has p-1 elements because we exclude 0 which has no inverse. Remember: 'Prime Leaves p-1 survivors'.

mnemonics.title: Generator Count
mnemonics.content: Number of generators in Zp* = φ(p-1). Think: 'Phi of p-1 finds primitive root count'.

acronyms.expansion: Multiplicative group of integers modulo p (non-zero residues)
acronyms.usage: Used in public-key cryptography, primality testing
Group of all permutations of size n, order n!
Important in group theory, has order n!/2
Used in modular arithmetic and cryptography (RSA)
Used to find order of permutation from cycle decomposition

visualTips.visualization: Think of cycle (a b c) as a rotation: a→b→c→a. Draw as a circle with arrows showing where each element maps.
Picture a clock with p-1 hours (excluding 0). Multiplication mod p wraps around the clock. Generators visit all positions.
Parity can be visualized by counting 'inversions' - pairs out of order. Even inversions = even permutation.
Every group G can be 'represented' as permutation group - think of each element as permuting the group elements (left multiplication).

analogies.analogy: Like a clock where after p-1 steps you return to starting position (generator behavior). Each non-zero number is like a position on this mathematical clock.
Like shuffling a deck of cards - each shuffle is a permutation. The set of all possible shuffles forms the symmetric group.
Like finding independent cycles in a dance party - some dancers move in 2-cycles, some in 3-cycles, etc. The whole dance is combination of these independent cycles.
Like only allowing 'fair' shuffles - those requiring even number of card swaps. Half of all possible shuffles are fair.
